meta.title: 1.1 The Prompt-to-Code Product Category
meta.description: Distinguish code assistants, code generators, and coding agents by their architecture and user intent — and understand why Make spans the generator-to-agent boundary in a way that makes it harder to build but more defensible than any single-category competitor.

blocks.prompt:
> Name every AI coding tool you can think of. Now try to categorize them — what axis would you use to distinguish them? (Hint: it's not "good vs. bad.")

---

blocks.content:
## What You'll Learn

This module gives you a three-category taxonomy — assistants, generators, agents — and explains why the competitive battle in this market is not about model quality but about who owns the workflow layer. By the end, you'll be able to explain where Make sits in this taxonomy, what the "last-mile problem" is and why it can't be closed by better models alone, and why every major player is accumulating surface area toward a platform play.

The AI coding market divides into assistants, generators, and agents — and the competitive battle is not about model quality but about who owns the workflow layer that connects design, generation, deployment, and iteration. The surface similarity of these products obscures completely different bets on architecture, user intent, and business model. A Platform PM who can't distinguish these categories cannot make prioritization arguments, cannot position Make credibly to partners or stakeholders, and cannot anticipate where the market is heading.

**The Category Taxonomy**

**Code Assistants** augment human coding. They sit inside an existing IDE (Integrated Development Environment — the desktop application developers write code in, like VS Code or JetBrains) and make the developer faster. They do not generate complete applications — they autocomplete functions, explain errors, suggest refactors, and answer questions about the codebase.

Examples: GitHub Copilot, JetBrains AI Assistant, Tabnine, Codeium, Amazon Q Developer.

The user remains in control of the code. The assistant is a tool, not an agent. Copilot is not trying to build your app — it's trying to write the next line of code you were going to write anyway. This category is deep in the developer workflow, high adoption, relatively low controversy (low risk per suggestion), and commoditizing rapidly.

**Code Generators** accept a prompt and produce a working artifact — a component, a page, an app. The user does not need to read the code to get value. They care about the output (does it look right? does it work?) not the implementation.

Examples: Vercel v0, Lovable, Bolt (StackBlitz), Framer AI, Anima.

The user delegates implementation. The generator is trying to ship something useful in one prompt. These products are primarily UI/frontend-focused because visual output is verifiable without reading code. "Does this look like what I described?" is easier to evaluate than "Is this backend correctly structured?"

**Coding Agents** can plan, act across multiple steps, use tools (file system, browser, terminal, APIs), and iterate without per-step human approval. They are not just writing code — they are doing software engineering.

Examples: Replit Agent, Devin (Cognition), Claude Code, OpenHands, SWE-agent.

The user delegates a goal, not a task. "Build me a SaaS (Software-as-a-Service) app with user auth, a dashboard, and Stripe billing" is an agent prompt. The agent may take 20+ steps, run tests, read error messages, fix bugs, and verify functionality. The risk is higher (agents can break things), the trust requirement is higher, and the value ceiling is higher.

**Where Make Fits**

Make spans the Code Generator and early Coding Agent categories. The prompt-to-working-app flow is generator territory. The agent infrastructure (tool calls, iterative refinement, error recovery) is agent territory. Make's unique position: it is the only tool where generation happens inside the design canvas, with design system awareness baked in.

**What Users Actually Want**

Users do not want code. They want a working product. This distinction sounds trivial but has large product implications.

A user who wants code is a developer who wants help writing it faster — Copilot's market. A user who wants a working product wants something that runs, looks right, and can be shared — Make's market. The product quality bar for "working product" is much higher than for "code that compiles." It encompasses: visual correctness, interaction fidelity, data loading, error states, empty states, responsive layout, and performance.

This is why Make is harder to build than Copilot. Copilot is judged on one line at a time. Make is judged on the whole experience.

blocks.question: A PM candidate says "the AI code-gen market is winner-take-all because whoever has the best model wins." What's wrong with this analysis, and what does it miss about the competitive dynamics?
blocks.answer: Model quality is a shared, rapidly commoditizing resource — all players access the same frontier models (GPT-4, Claude, Gemini) via API. The competitive moat is not model quality but: (1) data flywheels (proprietary training data from user interactions), (2) integration depth (how tightly the tool is embedded in an existing workflow), (3) distribution (existing user base, enterprise sales motion), and (4) platform lock-in (the more your project lives in the tool, the harder it is to leave). 'Best model wins' is a 2022 framing. In 2025, the battle is for workflow ownership.

blocks.content:
**The Last-Mile Problem**

The "last mile" is the gap between generated code and a working app. It appears in every product in this category:
- Generated code compiles but throws runtime errors
- Component renders but with wrong spacing
- Data fetching works but loading states are missing
- Form submits but validation is skipped
- App works on desktop but breaks on mobile

AI models are excellent at generating plausible code. They are poor at testing it, simulating edge cases, and verifying visual correctness. The last mile requires a quality layer — either human review (trusted by developers, unscalable for non-technical users) or automated eval (what Make's eval infrastructure — the system that automatically tests generated output against quality criteria — must build).

## How to Think About This

Model quality is a commodity in this market — all major players access the same frontier models (GPT-4, Claude, Gemini) via API. The companies winning are winning on workflow ownership: how deeply embedded in the user's existing process, how much project state is locked in, how tightly the output connects to the next step. Think of it as a pipeline: the tool that owns the most steps in the pipeline (design → generate → preview → deploy → iterate) has the strongest retention and the hardest-to-replicate moat. Model improvements are table stakes; pipeline position is the durable advantage.

**Market Size and Growth**

The developer tools market is approximately $30B globally. AI code generation is the fastest-growing segment, growing from near-zero in 2021 to multiple-billion-dollar ARR (Annual Recurring Revenue — the annualized value of subscription contracts) across the category in 2025. Copilot alone crossed $1B ARR. The non-developer segment (non-technical builders using tools like Bolt and Lovable) is growing faster than the developer segment.

The long-run question is not "how big is the market" but "who owns the platform." Copilot owns the IDE layer. Vercel owns the deployment layer. Figma could own the design-to-code layer. These are not competing — they are potentially stacked, and the platform that connects them captures the most value.

**The Convergence Toward Platform Plays**

Every major player in this space is becoming a platform. Copilot added extensions. Replit became a full cloud IDE. Vercel launched v0 and then Build. StackBlitz built the WebContainers SDK. The tools are accumulating surface area because the value is not in any one tool — it is in the integration across the workflow. Make's platform strategy must answer: what is the workflow that Make owns end-to-end, and where does it hand off to others?

---

blocks.question: Every major player in this space is described as 'becoming a platform.' What does that actually mean — and why does the direction of accumulation (Copilot adding extensions, Replit becoming a full IDE) matter for how Make defines its own platform boundaries?
blocks.answer: Becoming a platform means moving from solving one specific problem to owning the connections between multiple workflow steps — which creates lock-in and network effects that a point solution can't match. The direction of accumulation matters because it reveals where each player believes the durable value lives: Copilot is moving from line completion toward full-context codebase understanding; Replit is moving from an IDE toward a full cloud compute and deployment service. For Make, the direction is already set by Figma's asset: design-to-code. The platform bet is that owning the design layer is the most defensible position for an AI code-gen platform, because design system context is upstream of every other workflow step. Make should accumulate surface area toward deployment and iteration — not toward IDE features that Cursor already owns.

blocks.title:
Spend 15 minutes each with v0, Bolt, and Replit Agent (use the free tiers). Give each the same prompt: "Build a task management app with a list, a form to add tasks, and the ability to mark tasks complete." Compare: What framework was generated? How was state managed? Did it work on first try? What broke? How long until you had something you'd show to a user?

---
blocks.solution: Across v0, Bolt, and Replit Agent, you'll see the same prompt produce meaningfully different outputs. v0 will generate a Next.js component with TypeScript and shadcn/ui — well-structured, production-looking, but it won't run without a Next.js project wrapping it. Bolt will generate a complete, runnable React app with Vite in under 30 seconds, with the preview running directly in the browser — often working on first try for a simple CRUD app. Replit Agent will open a terminal, scaffold a full project, potentially install dependencies, and may iterate on errors automatically — taking 2–5 minutes but often producing the most complete result. Key observations: (1) v0 produces the highest-quality code but requires the most developer scaffolding to run; (2) Bolt is fastest to a working demo but may have rough edges in state management; (3) Replit Agent takes longest but shows genuine agentic behavior (you can watch it read errors and self-correct). The 'last mile' failures you'll commonly see: missing empty state when the task list is empty, no persistence after page refresh unless you explicitly asked for localStorage, form that doesn't clear after submission. These omissions are consistent across all three — they reveal the last-mile gap that every platform in this category faces.

blocks.prompt:
Explain the three-category taxonomy (assistants, generators, agents) to a Figma product designer who asked "what makes Make different from Copilot?" Use one concrete difference per category.

---

questions: Think of a time you used or evaluated an AI tool that seemed to solve your problem — until it didn't. What was the last-mile failure that made it fall short, and which of the three categories (assistant, generator, agent) best describes what you actually needed in that moment?

Recall a product decision where you had to choose between building for the power user (who can bridge the last mile themselves) and the non-technical user (who cannot). How did you resolve it, and how does that experience translate to how Make should handle its trust gradient?

---

blocks.content:
The AI coding market divides into assistants (augment humans), generators (produce artifacts from prompts), and agents (achieve goals autonomously) — and the competitive battle is not about model quality but about who owns the workflow layer that connects design, generation, deployment, and iteration.

---

blocks.prompt:
> If the taxonomy tells you what each product does, the architecture map tells you how. v0, Lovable, Bolt, and Replit Agent all take prompts and produce apps — but the engineering underneath them is completely different. That's next.

---

---

reviewQuestions.question: Which of the following correctly defines the difference between a 'Code Assistant' and a 'Code Generator'?
options: Code Assistants generate entire applications from prompts; Code Generators only write individual functions or suggest next lines

Code Assistants augment human developers inside an existing IDE — suggesting completions and explaining code; Code Generators accept a prompt and produce a complete working artifact without requiring the user to read the code
Code Assistants are free tools while Code Generators are enterprise products — the distinction is about pricing, not capability
reviewQuestions.explanation: The taxonomy is defined by what the user delegates and where the tool lives. Code Assistants (like GitHub Copilot) sit inside an IDE and help developers write code faster — the developer remains in control, reading and owning every line. Code Generators (like v0, Lovable, Bolt) accept a prompt and produce a complete artifact; the user evaluates the output by looking at it, not reading the code. The user intent and delegation level are completely different.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Good — the delegation model is the defining distinction. Assistants help you write; generators produce the artifact for you.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: The distinction isn't about who generates more code or about pricing. It's about delegation: Code Assistants help a developer write code line by line — the developer stays in control. Code Generators accept a goal and produce the artifact — the user delegates implementation entirely. These represent fundamentally different user intents and workflows.

reviewQuestions.question: What is the 'last-mile problem' in AI code generation?
options: The gap between code that compiles without errors and a working product — including runtime errors, missing empty states, broken mobile layouts, and absent error handling
The latency between when a user submits a prompt and when generated code appears in the preview
The difficulty of deploying AI-generated code to production hosting platforms
reviewQuestions.explanation: The last mile is the distance between 'code that runs' and 'a working product.' AI models produce plausible code that compiles, but a production-quality product also requires: graceful error states, loading states, empty states, mobile responsiveness, input validation, and behavior under unexpected conditions. These gaps don't cause compile errors — they only appear under real conditions. Closing the last mile requires either human review or automated evaluation infrastructure.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Exactly — the last mile is specifically the gap between code that compiles and an experience that actually works under real conditions.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: The last mile isn't about generation latency or deployment steps. It's the quality gap between 'code that compiles' and 'a product that works.' AI models handle syntax well but miss the cases that only fail under real conditions: error states, edge case inputs, mobile breakage, missing loading states. That gap is the last mile.

reviewQuestions.question: Why is 'best model wins' no longer an accurate framing of competitive advantage in AI code generation as of 2025?
options: The argument is correct for developer tools but incorrect for non-technical users who care more about UX than model capability
Model quality has plateaued — all frontier models produce identical output quality, so differences no longer matter

Frontier models are available via API to any competitor, so model quality is a shared resource — the durable competitive advantages are workflow ownership, distribution, integration depth, and data flywheels
reviewQuestions.explanation: GPT-4, Claude, and Gemini are all available via API to any team with a credit card. Model quality is not a proprietary advantage — it is a commodity. The companies winning in this market are winning on workflow position: how many steps of the design-to-deploy pipeline they own, how deeply embedded their tool is in existing workflows, and what proprietary data flywheels they've built from user interactions. Make's Figma distribution is a better moat than any model improvement.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Right — workflow ownership and distribution are the durable moats, and understanding this is the key reframe the module is making.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Model quality hasn't plateaued, and the argument is flawed for all segments, not just one. The core issue is that frontier models are available to everyone via API — they're not proprietary. The companies winning in code generation are winning on workflow position and distribution, not model quality. Figma's 4 million paying customers is a better moat than any particular model version.
